  2. Writing Specs

Writing Effective Specs

The better your PROMPT.md, the better Ralph's output. Learn how to write specs that get results.

01

The Spec Template

Start every PROMPT.md with this structure

A good spec has three essential parts: what you want, how it should work, and what constraints to follow. Here's a template you can copy and customize:

markdown PROMPT.md Template 
# [Feature/Task Title]

## Goal
[One sentence describing what you want to accomplish]

## Context
[Brief background on why this is needed - helps the AI understand intent]

## Requirements
- [Requirement 1: Be specific about behavior]
- [Requirement 2: Include success criteria]
- [Requirement 3: Add as many as needed]

## Technical Details
- [Tech stack preferences if any]
- [Existing patterns to follow]
- [Files that will be affected]

## Constraints
- [What NOT to do]
- [Performance requirements]
- [Security considerations]

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] [Checkbox 1: How to verify it works]
- [ ] [Checkbox 2: Edge case to test]
- [ ] [Checkbox 3: Expected output]

## Notes (Optional)
[Any additional context, references, or considerations]

Tips for Better Specs

  • Be specific: "Add a button" is vague. "Add a blue submit button below the form that calls the /api/submit endpoint" is clear.
  • Include context: Tell Ralph why you need this. It helps the AI make better decisions.
  • Set boundaries: The Constraints section prevents Ralph from over-engineering or touching code it shouldn't.
  • Define success: Acceptance criteria let Ralph verify its own work matches your expectations.
02

Example Specs

Real-world examples for different skill levels

Developer Example

API Authentication Endpoint

A technical spec with architecture decisions and implementation details

markdown PROMPT.md 
# Add JWT Authentication to API

## Goal
Implement JWT-based authentication for the REST API with refresh token support.

## Context
The API currently has no authentication. We need to secure endpoints before
the beta launch. Users will authenticate via email/password and receive
JWT tokens for subsequent requests.

## Requirements
- POST /api/auth/login - Accept email/password, return access + refresh tokens
- POST /api/auth/refresh - Accept refresh token, return new access token
- POST /api/auth/logout - Invalidate refresh token
- Access tokens expire in 15 minutes
- Refresh tokens expire in 7 days
- Store refresh tokens in Redis with user ID mapping

## Technical Details
- Use jsonwebtoken library (already in package.json)
- Follow existing controller pattern in src/controllers/
- Add auth middleware to src/middleware/auth.ts
- Use existing Redis client from src/lib/redis.ts
- Hash passwords with bcrypt (cost factor 12)

## Constraints
- Do NOT modify existing user routes
- Do NOT add rate limiting (separate task)
- Keep backward compatibility - unauthenticated endpoints stay open for now
- No external auth providers (OAuth is a future task)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Login returns valid JWT that decodes correctly
- [ ] Protected routes reject requests without valid token
- [ ] Refresh flow works and invalidates old refresh token
- [ ] Logout invalidates refresh token in Redis
- [ ] Invalid credentials return 401, not 500
- [ ] Tokens include user ID and role in payload

## Notes
Refer to RFC 7519 for JWT best practices. The frontend team expects
tokens in the response body, not cookies.

Why This Works

  • Clear endpoints: Each API route is specified with expected behavior
  • Technical precision: Libraries, file paths, and patterns are explicit
  • Boundaries set: Constraints prevent scope creep
  • Verifiable: Acceptance criteria are testable
Vibe Coder Example

Add Dark Mode Toggle

A plain-language spec that describes intent without deep technical details

markdown PROMPT.md 
# Add Dark Mode to the App

## Goal
Let users switch between light and dark themes.

## Context
Users have been asking for dark mode. The app currently only has a light theme.
I want a simple toggle that remembers the user's preference.

## Requirements
- Add a toggle button somewhere visible (header or settings)
- Dark mode should have a dark background and light text
- Remember the user's choice when they come back
- Respect the user's system preference by default

## Constraints
- Keep the existing color scheme for light mode
- Don't make it too complicated - just light and dark, no custom themes
- Make sure text is still readable in both modes

## What Success Looks Like
- [ ] I can click a button to switch between light and dark
- [ ] When I refresh the page, my choice is remembered
- [ ] If I've never chosen, it uses my system setting
- [ ] Everything is still readable and looks good

Why This Works

  • Clear intent: The goal is obvious even without technical details
  • User perspective: Written from the user's point of view
  • Guardrails: Constraints prevent over-engineering
  • Flexible: Lets Ralph make technical decisions within boundaries
03

Spec Checklist

Run through this before running Ralph

Must Have

Should Have

Common Mistakes

  • ✕ Vague requirements: "Make it better"
  • ✕ Too broad scope: "Rebuild the whole app"
  • ✕ Missing constraints: No boundaries
  • ✕ Conflicting requirements
  • ✕ No success criteria: "Just make it work"
04

Advanced Tips

Level up your spec writing

Break Large Tasks Down

Instead of one massive PROMPT.md, create multiple smaller specs. Ralph works better with focused tasks. "Add user authentication" is better as 3 separate specs: registration, login, and password reset.

Include File Paths

Tell Ralph exactly which files to modify: "Update src/components/Header.tsx" is clearer than "update the header." This prevents Ralph from creating new files when it should edit existing ones.

Reference Existing Patterns

If your codebase has patterns, point to them: "Follow the same pattern as src/controllers/UserController.ts". Ralph will learn from your existing code.

Specify What to Keep

Sometimes what NOT to change is as important as what to change. "Keep the existing error handling" or "Don't modify the database schema" prevents surprises.

Add Examples When Helpful

For data transformations or APIs, include input/output examples. "Given input: {name: 'John'}, expect output: 'Hello, John!'" removes ambiguity.

Use More Iterations for Complex Tasks

Simple fixes? Use -Q (Quick, 3 passes). Complex features? Use -T (Thorough, 10 passes) or -L (Long, 30 passes). More iterations = more refinement.

Ready to write your first spec?

Copy the template, customize it for your task, and let Ralph do the rest.

Ralph Workflow

Multi-agent AI orchestration for automated code development. Ship your ideas with autonomous agents that collaborate until quality is achieved.
